summary: DeVault explains why he has been developing the Hare programming language in a semi-private state for over two years despite it being fully open source. He argues that keeping the community small during development allows breaking changes to be managed easily, since one person can update every downstream project. Privacy protects new discoverers from encountering an incomplete language and losing enthusiasm, and ensures the first public impression is of a polished, production-ready tool. He clarifies that 'open source' means licensing, not public visibility, and asks readers to respect the team's privacy. The post's update reveals that readers immediately violated this request, leaking the project on Hacker News and Lobsters.
key_points: A small, private community allows breaking changes to be propagated across every downstream project by a single person, which is impossible at scale
Premature public attention creates a support burden from users who lack the expertise to work through rough edges, draining developer bandwidth
Open source licensing and public development are distinct concepts — a project can be fully free software while being developed semi-privately
First impressions matter enormously for programming languages; launching incomplete invites permanent dismissal rather than curiosity
The update reveals the internet's inability to respect a sincere request for patience, with readers immediately sharing and critiquing the unfinished project
money_quotes: This language feels like the language I always wanted, and that's mission accomplished by any definition I care to consider.

Often, the person who implements a breaking change will also send patches to various downstreams updating them to be compatible with this change, for every extant software project written in the language.
I want to fast-forward to the phase where people study it to learn how to apply it to their problems, rather than to learn if they can apply it to their problems.
"Open source" does not mean that the project has a public face.

Every person who has worked on the project, and every person who stumbles upon it, possesses the right to lift the veil of secrecy and share it with the world. The reason they don't is because I asked them not to.
A new programming language is a major undertaking. We're building one with an explicit emphasis on simplicity and we're still not done after two years.
This post was not a marketing stunt. It was a heartfelt appeal to your better nature.
I had hoped that you would help relieve some of that stress by taking me seriously when I explained my motivations and asked nicely for you to leave us be. I was wrong.
key_insight: Developing a programming language semi-privately preserves the ability to make fearless breaking changes and ensures the first public impression is of a complete, usable tool rather than an incomplete experiment.
